title: The Unit Circle
blurb: Radians as wrapped radii, sine and cosine as the coordinates of a point walking the rim, the exact-value angles worth memorizing, and reference angles that unlock all four quadrants.
buildsOn: Right-triangle trig ratios (Geometry); the coordinate plane.

lessons.title: Radians
lessons.concreteComparison: A radian is measuring an angle with the circle's own ruler: peel off one radius, bend it along the rim — the angle it spans is 1 radian. A full lap swallows 2π of them, because the circumference is 2π radii.
lessons.objective: Define the radian via arc length, convert between degrees and radians, and compute arc lengths with s = rθ.
concept: Degrees are arbitrary (Babylonian 360); **radians** measure angles with the circle's own unit: one radian is the angle whose arc is exactly one RADIUS long. Since the circumference is $2\pi r$ — the radius fits around $2\pi \approx 6.28$ times — a full turn is $2\pi$ radians.

The exchange rate follows: $360° = 2\pi$, so $180° = \pi$ — the master conversion. Multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$ to go into radians, by $\frac{180}{\pi}$ to come back: $90° = \frac{\pi}{2}$, $60° = \frac{\pi}{3}$, $45° = \frac{\pi}{4}$, $30° = \frac{\pi}{6}$.

The payoff: in radians, arc length is just $s = r\theta$ — angle times radius, no conversion factor. (In degrees it needs the clunky $\frac{\pi}{180}$ patch.) This is why calculus and physics run entirely on radians: the circle's native unit makes the formulas clean.
lessons.keyIdea: One radian = arc of one radius; full turn = 2π; convert via 180° = π; and in radians, arc length is simply s = rθ.

facts.title: Master conversion
facts.explanation: Multiply by π/180 (to rad) or 180/π (to deg).

facts.title: The lap
facts.explanation: The radius wraps the rim 2π times — a little more than 6.

facts.title: Arc length
facts.explanation: Radians make the arc formula multiplication, nothing more.

workedExamples.statement: Convert $135°$ to radians and $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ to degrees.
A pendulum of length 2 m swings through $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ radians. How long is the arc its tip traces? What would the same swing be in degrees?
workedExamples.solution: $s = r\theta = 2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.05$ m. In degrees: $\frac{\pi}{6} = 30°$ — and notice the degree version of the arc formula would need the $\frac{\pi}{180}$ patch to get the same answer.

commonMistakes.correction: $\pi$ is a NUMBER, about 3.14; the statement is $\pi$ RADIANS $= 180°$ — an equation between two units, like 1 inch = 2.54 cm. Keep the unit in mind or conversions start multiplying the wrong way.

commonMistakes.text: Using s = rθ with θ in degrees.
commonMistakes.correction: The clean formula is radian-only. With degrees you must first convert: $s = r \cdot \theta° \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}$. If an arc answer looks 57× too big, this is why.

tryIt.statement: Convert $240°$ and $-45°$ to radians, and $\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$ to degrees.
A circular track has radius 50 m. A runner covers an arc of 125 m. What central angle (in radians, then degrees) did they run through, using $\frac{180}{\pi} \approx 57.3$?

steps.title: Measure the circle with itself
steps.body: Degrees are a historical accident — Babylon liked 360. Mathematics prefers a unit the circle chooses for itself: peel off one RADIUS, bend it along the rim, and call the angle it spans **one radian**. No culture, no convention: just the circle measuring itself.

steps.title: How many radii around?
steps.teach: Watch the circle roll one full turn, unrolling its rim into a straight line. The rim is $2\pi$ radii long — a little more than 6. That number IS why a full turn is $2\pi$ radians: you just watched π get defined.

steps.title: Wrap radii onto the rim
steps.teach: Now wrap radius-arcs around the rim one at a time: 1 radian, 2, 3… A half-turn swallows π ≈ 3.14 of them, the full lap 2π ≈ 6.28. Feel how big one radian is: about 57°, a generous slice.

steps.title: The master exchange rate
steps.body: One equation carries every conversion: $180° = \pi$ radians. Going TO radians, multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$; coming BACK, by $\frac{180}{\pi}$. The famous angles become clean fractions of π: $90° = \frac{\pi}{2}$, $60° = \frac{\pi}{3}$, $45° = \frac{\pi}{4}$, $30° = \frac{\pi}{6}$ — learn these as vocabulary, not calculations.

steps.title: Instant vocabulary
steps.explanation: $\frac{2\pi}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 180° = 120°$. Reading fractions of π as fractions of 180° makes these instant.

steps.title: Arc length becomes multiplication
steps.body: Why bother switching units? Because radians make geometry ALGEBRAIC: the arc subtended by angle $\theta$ on a circle of radius $r$ is simply $s = r\theta$. One radian on a 5-m circle: 5 m of arc. Two radians: 10 m. In degrees the same formula needs a $\frac{\pi}{180}$ patch. Every formula downstream (angular velocity, calculus derivatives of sine) stays clean ONLY in radians.

steps.title: Solve s = rθ backwards
steps.prompt: An arc of 12 cm sits on a circle of radius 4 cm. The central angle is:
steps.explanation: $\theta = \frac{s}{r} = \frac{12}{4} = 3$ radians (about 172°). Dividing arc by radius — literally counting how many radii fit — is what a radian measure IS.

steps.title: Anchor to the quarter-lap
steps.body: Orient any radian value instantly against the compass points: $\frac{\pi}{2}$ = quarter lap (up), $\pi$ = half (left), $\frac{3\pi}{2}$ = three-quarters (down), $2\pi$ = home. Is $\frac{5\pi}{6}$ big or small? Just under π — nearly half a lap, second quadrant. No conversion needed for orientation.

steps.title: The τ rebellion
steps.body: Some mathematicians argue the circle constant should be $\tau = 2\pi \approx 6.28$, so a full turn is exactly 1τ, a quarter turn $\frac{\tau}{4}$ — no factor-of-2 bookkeeping. Tau Day (June 28) celebrates it. π's 300-year head start seems safe, but the debate is genuinely about making radians even MORE natural.

steps.title: What sticks
points: 1 radian = the angle cut by one radius of arc; full turn = 2π ≈ 6.28.
Convert via 180° = π; know the family π/6, π/4, π/3, π/2 by heart.
s = rθ (radians only) — the clean formula is the whole point of the unit.

lessons.title: Sine & Cosine on the Circle
lessons.concreteComparison: Put a bug on a ferris wheel of radius 1. At every moment, cosine is the bug's shadow on the ground (x-coordinate) and sine is its height on the wall (y-coordinate). Trig stops being about triangles and becomes about a point going around.
lessons.objective: Define cos θ and sin θ as the coordinates of the point at angle θ on the unit circle, extend trig beyond 90°, and derive the Pythagorean identity.
concept: The **unit circle** definition: stand at $(1, 0)$, rotate counterclockwise by angle $\theta$; the point you reach has coordinates $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Cosine IS the x-coordinate, sine IS the y-coordinate — for ANY angle: bigger than 90°, negative, beyond a full lap.

This extends right-triangle trig seamlessly: in the first quadrant, the point, its shadow on the x-axis, and the center form a right triangle with hypotenuse 1 — adjacent $= \cos\theta$, opposite $= \sin\theta$, the old SOH-CAH-TOA. The circle just keeps going where triangles must stop.

The point sits on the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 1$, so $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ — the **Pythagorean identity**, true for every angle, the single most-used equation in trigonometry. Given one of sine/cosine (and a quadrant), it recovers the other.
lessons.keyIdea: The point at angle θ on the unit circle is (cos θ, sin θ) — cosine is x, sine is y, for any θ — and living on x² + y² = 1 forces cos²θ + sin²θ = 1.

facts.title: The definition
facts.explanation: Rotate from (1, 0) by θ; read the coordinates.

facts.title: Anchors
facts.explanation: The four compass points give the easiest exact values.

facts.title: Pythagorean identity
facts.explanation: The circle's equation, worn as an identity.

workedExamples.solution: Half a lap lands at $(-1, 0)$: $\cos\pi = -1$, $\sin\pi = 0$. Three quarters lands at $(0, -1)$: $\cos\frac{3\pi}{2} = 0$, $\sin\frac{3\pi}{2} = -1$. No triangles required — just read coordinates.

workedExamples.statement: An angle $\theta$ in the second quadrant has $\sin\theta = \tfrac{3}{5}$. Find $\cos\theta$.
workedExamples.solution: Identity: $\cos^2\theta = 1 - \frac{9}{25} = \frac{16}{25}$, so $\cos\theta = \pm\frac{4}{5}$. Second quadrant means x-coordinates are NEGATIVE: $\cos\theta = -\frac{4}{5}$.

commonMistakes.text: Refusing sin 150° because 'a right triangle can't have a 150° angle.'
commonMistakes.correction: The CIRCLE definition doesn't need a triangle: rotate 150° and read the y-coordinate (it's $+\frac{1}{2}$). Triangles were the training wheels; the circle is the definition.

commonMistakes.text: Writing cos²θ + sin²θ = 1 only for acute θ, or 'fixing' signs: cos²θ − sin²θ.
commonMistakes.correction: Squares erase signs — the identity holds for EVERY angle in every quadrant, verbatim. It is the circle equation $x^2 + y^2 = 1$; no angle escapes the circle.

tryIt.solution: $(1,0)$: $\cos 0 = 1$. $(0,1)$: $\sin\frac{\pi}{2} = 1$. Full lap returns home: $\cos 2\pi = 1$. Quarter lap CLOCKWISE lands at $(0,-1)$: $\sin(-\frac{\pi}{2}) = -1$.

tryIt.statement: $\cos\theta = -\tfrac{5}{13}$ with $\theta$ in the third quadrant. Find $\sin\theta$ and verify the identity.
tryIt.solution: $\sin^2\theta = 1 - \frac{25}{169} = \frac{144}{169}$: $\sin\theta = \pm\frac{12}{13}$. Third quadrant: y is negative — $\sin\theta = -\frac{12}{13}$. Check: $\frac{25}{169} + \frac{144}{169} = 1$. ✓

steps.title: From triangles to a ferris wheel
steps.body: Right-triangle trig dies at 90° — no right triangle holds a bigger angle. The fix is beautiful: put the angle at the center of a circle of radius 1, rotate a point from $(1, 0)$, and DEFINE cosine and sine as that point's coordinates: $P(\theta) = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Now any angle works: 150°, 300°, $-\frac{\pi}{2}$, three full laps.

steps.title: Ride the rim
steps.teach: Step the angle and watch the point orbit. The inner right triangle shows the old story — adjacent = x, opposite = y — but keep stepping past 90°: the triangle flips into new quadrants while the coordinates simply keep reporting $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$, signs and all.

steps.title: The four compass points
steps.body: Four angles need no computation — just look where the point lands. $\theta = 0$: $(1, 0)$. Quarter lap $\frac{\pi}{2}$: $(0, 1)$. Half lap $\pi$: $(-1, 0)$. Three quarters $\frac{3\pi}{2}$: $(0, -1)$. Every cos/sin of these is 0, 1, or −1, read straight off the axes. These anchors calibrate everything else.

steps.title: Signs by quadrant
steps.prompt: For an angle in the THIRD quadrant (between π and 3π/2), the signs of (cos θ, sin θ) are:
steps.explanation: Third-quadrant points sit down-left: x negative, y negative. Cosine and sine INHERIT the coordinate signs — no memorization beyond knowing the plane.

steps.title: The circle equation in disguise
steps.body: The point $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ lives on the unit circle, whose equation is $x^2 + y^2 = 1$. Substitute: $$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1.$$ This **Pythagorean identity** holds for every angle without exception — it is the most-used equation in all of trigonometry. Practical power: know one of sine/cosine, and the identity yields the other (up to a sign the quadrant settles).

steps.title: Use the identity
steps.prompt: $\sin\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}$ and $\theta$ is in the second quadrant. Then $\cos\theta =$
steps.explanation: $\cos^2\theta = 1 - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{4}$, so $\cos\theta = \pm\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$; quadrant II makes x negative. Identity for the size, quadrant for the sign.

steps.title: One angle, four quadrant mirrors
steps.teach: The four points at $(\pm\frac{4}{5}, \pm\frac{3}{5})$ all sit on the unit circle (check: $\frac{16}{25} + \frac{9}{25} = 1$). Same triangle shape, four sign patterns — the quadrant decides which signs cosine and sine wear. Next lesson turns this picture into a method.

steps.title: Sketch the point first
steps.body: Before evaluating anything trig, mark roughly where the angle's point sits on the circle. The sketch hands you both signs and a size sanity-check ('$\sin 200°$ should be small and negative') before any formula runs. Ten seconds of drawing beats every sign-memorization scheme ever invented.

steps.title: What sticks
points: P(θ) = (cos θ, sin θ): cosine is x, sine is y, for every angle.
cos²θ + sin²θ = 1 always; identity gives the size, quadrant gives the sign.

lessons.title: Special Angles & Exact Values
lessons.concreteComparison: Three numbers — ½, √2/2, √3/2 — are trig's entire multiplication table. They come from folding two tiles: a square cut along its diagonal (45°) and an equilateral triangle cut down its spine (30°–60°). Every exact value on the circle is one of these three, dressed in a sign.
lessons.objective: Derive the exact sine/cosine values at 30°, 45°, 60° from the two special triangles, place them on the first-quadrant circle, and use the size ordering to self-check.
concept: Two folded tiles generate everything. **The half-square** (45–45–90): legs 1, hypotenuse $\sqrt{2}$ — scaled to hypotenuse 1, both legs are $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$: $\cos 45° = \sin 45° = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$. **The half-equilateral** (30–60–90): sides $1, \sqrt{3}, 2$ — scaled down, $\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \sin 30° = \frac{1}{2}$, and 60° swaps them.

The three values in size order: $\frac{1}{2} = 0.5 < \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.707 < \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$. As the angle climbs 30° → 45° → 60°, sine climbs through them (the point rises) while cosine descends the same list (the point moves left). One list, two directions.

In radians the family is $\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{3}$ — with the compass anchors 0 and $\frac{\pi}{2}$ bracketing them. These five first-quadrant angles, plus reference-angle logic (next lesson), generate every exact value on the whole circle.
lessons.keyIdea: Two triangles give three values — 1/2, √2/2, √3/2; sine climbs the list as the angle grows, cosine descends it, and 30°/60° swap each other's values.

facts.title: The table
facts.explanation: Three values; sine reads up, cosine reads down.

facts.title: Sources
facts.explanation: Half a square; half an equilateral triangle.

workedExamples.statement: Derive $\cos 30°$ and $\sin 30°$ from the equilateral triangle, exactly.
workedExamples.solution: Fold an equilateral triangle of side 2 down its spine: the half-triangle has hypotenuse 2, short leg 1 (half the base), and spine $\sqrt{4 - 1} = \sqrt{3}$. At the 30° apex angle: opposite is the short leg: $\sin 30° = \frac{1}{2}$; adjacent is the spine: $\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. Identity check: $\frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{4} = 1$. ✓

workedExamples.statement: Give the exact coordinates of the unit-circle point at $\theta = \tfrac{\pi}{4}$, and verify it lies on the circle.
workedExamples.solution: $45°$: both coordinates $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$: $P = \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)$ — the diagonal point, x = y. On the circle: $\frac{2}{4} + \frac{2}{4} = 1$. ✓

commonMistakes.correction: At 30° the point has barely risen — sine must be the SMALL value, $\frac{1}{2}$. Picture the point: low and far right means small sine, big cosine. The size picture beats the memorized table.

commonMistakes.text: Writing √2/2 as '1/√2 simplified to 2/√2' or other mangled forms.
commonMistakes.correction: $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ — multiply top and bottom by $\sqrt{2}$. Both forms are correct; the mangled middle isn't. When in doubt, square it: a true form squares to $\frac{1}{2}$.

tryIt.statement: From memory (then verify with the identity): $\sin 60°$, $\cos 60°$, and the exact coordinates at $\theta = \tfrac{\pi}{3}$.
A ramp rises at 30° for 12 m along its slope. Using exact values, how high does it climb and how far does it run horizontally?

steps.title: Three numbers to rule them all
steps.body: Calculators give decimals; mathematics wants EXACT values — and remarkably, the entire exact-value catalogue of trigonometry runs on three numbers: $\frac{1}{2}$, $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. They fall out of two triangles you can fold from paper: a square cut along its diagonal, and an equilateral triangle cut down its spine.

steps.title: The half-square: 45°
steps.body: Cut a unit square corner to corner: two 45–45–90 triangles, legs 1 and hypotenuse $\sqrt{2}$ (Pythagoras). Shrink so the hypotenuse is 1 (divide by $\sqrt{2}$): each leg becomes $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$. On the circle at 45°, adjacent and opposite are EQUAL: $\cos 45° = \sin 45° = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.707$ — the diagonal point where $x = y$.

steps.title: The half-equilateral: 30° and 60°
steps.body: Fold an equilateral triangle of side 2 down its spine: hypotenuse 2, short leg 1, spine $\sqrt{3}$ — the famous $1 : \sqrt{3} : 2$. Scaled to hypotenuse 1: the pair $\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. At 30° (low angle): $\sin = \frac{1}{2}$, $\cos = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. At 60° the SAME triangle stands on its other leg — the values swap.

steps.title: Visit the special angles
steps.teach: Step the angle to 30°, 45°, 60° and watch the coordinates hit the three sacred values. Notice the trade: as the point climbs (sine up through ½, .707, .866), it simultaneously slides left (cosine down the same list). One list, walked in both directions.

steps.title: The size test
steps.prompt: Without any table: which is LARGER, $\sin 40°$ or $\sin 50°$?
steps.explanation: Bigger first-quadrant angle = point farther around the rim = HIGHER: sine grows with the angle (until 90°). The picture answers instantly; no values needed.

steps.title: The first-quadrant ladder
steps.teach: The five first-quadrant landmarks, plotted (values ×1 on the unit circle): from $(1, 0)$ the point ladders up through the 30°, 45°, 60° stations to $(0, 1)$. Read sine as the rungs' heights: $0, \frac{1}{2}, 0.707, 0.866, 1$ — a staircase you can rebuild from two folded tiles.

steps.title: Which value?
steps.explanation: $\frac{\pi}{6} = 30°$: the point is far right, barely risen — cosine takes the BIG value $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. (Small angle: big cosine, small sine.)

steps.title: Rebuild in the margin
steps.body: Blank on an exact value mid-test? Don't guess — rebuild: sketch the half-square ($1, 1, \sqrt{2}$) or half-equilateral ($1, \sqrt{3}, 2$), scale to hypotenuse 1, read the legs. Twenty seconds, and it's derivation, not recall — immune to memory corruption.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Two tiles: half-square gives 45° (both √2/2); half-equilateral gives 30°/60° (½ and √3/2, swapping).
Sine climbs ½ → √2/2 → √3/2 as the angle grows; cosine walks the list backwards.
Blank? Rebuild the triangle — derive, don't guess.

lessons.title: Reference Angles & All Quadrants
lessons.concreteComparison: A reference angle is a mirror trick: the point at 150° is the 30° point reflected across the y-axis — same triangle, same numbers, different signs. Learn one quadrant, mirror it three times, and you own the whole circle.
lessons.objective: Find reference angles in every quadrant, attach signs via quadrant reasoning, and evaluate sine and cosine exactly for any special-family angle, including negative and oversized angles.
concept: The **reference angle** of $\theta$ is the acute angle between its terminal ray and the x-axis. The point at $\theta$ has the SAME |cos| and |sin| as its reference angle — only the SIGNS change, and the quadrant dictates them. Recipes: quadrant II: ref $= \pi - \theta$; III: ref $= \theta - \pi$; IV: ref $= 2\pi - \theta$.

Signs by quadrant, straight from coordinates: I $(+,+)$, II $(-,+)$, III $(-,-)$, IV $(+,-)$ for $(\cos, \sin)$. Two independent steps — size from the reference angle, sign from the quadrant — and any special-family angle falls: $\sin 150° = +\sin 30° = \frac{1}{2}$; $\cos\frac{4\pi}{3} = -\cos\frac{\pi}{3} = -\frac{1}{2}$.

Angles outside $[0, 2\pi)$ first come home by adding or subtracting full laps ($2\pi$ or $360°$): **coterminal angles** share the same point, so $\sin 420° = \sin 60°$ and $\cos(-\frac{\pi}{4}) = \cos\frac{7\pi}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.
lessons.keyIdea: Size from the reference angle (acute gap to the x-axis), sign from the quadrant, and oversized/negative angles come home first via full laps.

facts.title: Reference recipes
facts.explanation: The acute gap between the ray and the x-axis.

facts.title: Coterminal
facts.explanation: Strip full laps before anything else.

workedExamples.solution: $150°$ (QII): ref $= 30°$, sine positive in II: $+\frac{1}{2}$. $225°$ (QIII): ref $= 45°$, cosine negative: $-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$. $\frac{5\pi}{3} = 300°$ (QIV): ref $= 60°$, sine negative: $-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

Come home first: $495° - 360° = 135°$ (QII), ref $45°$, cosine negative: $-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$. $-\frac{\pi}{6}$: quarter-hour clockwise lands in QIV (same as $\frac{11\pi}{6}$), ref $\frac{\pi}{6}$, sine negative: $-\frac{1}{2}$.

commonMistakes.text: Measuring the reference angle from the y-axis: calling 150°'s reference 60°.
commonMistakes.correction: Reference angles are measured to the X-AXIS, always: $180° - 150° = 30°$. The y-axis version swaps every sine with cosine — a silent disaster. When unsure, sketch the drop to the horizontal.

commonMistakes.text: Letting the reference angle carry the sign: sin 210° = sin 30° because 'reference angles are equal.'
commonMistakes.correction: The reference angle only carries the SIZE. The sign belongs to the quadrant: 210° is QIII where sine is negative: $\sin 210° = -\frac{1}{2}$. Two separate steps, never merged.

tryIt.statement: Find the reference angle and evaluate: $\cos 120°$, $\sin\tfrac{7\pi}{6}$, $\cos\tfrac{7\pi}{4}$.
tryIt.solution: $120°$ (QII): ref $60°$, cos negative: $-\frac{1}{2}$. $\frac{7\pi}{6}$ (QIII): ref $\frac{\pi}{6}$, sin negative: $-\frac{1}{2}$. $\frac{7\pi}{4}$ (QIV): ref $\frac{\pi}{4}$, cos positive: $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.

tryIt.statement: Evaluate $\sin 780°$ and find ALL angles in $[0°, 360°)$ with $\cos\theta = -\tfrac{1}{2}$.
tryIt.solution: $780° - 720° = 60°$: $\sin 780° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. For $\cos = -\frac{1}{2}$: size says ref $= 60°$; negative cosine happens in QII and QIII: $\theta = 120°$ and $240°$.

steps.title: One quadrant, three mirrors
steps.body: You know five first-quadrant angles exactly. Here's the multiplier: every other special angle is one of those five REFLECTED into another quadrant. The point at 150° is the 30° point mirrored across the y-axis — same triangle, same $\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, different signs. The acute 'shadow angle' against the x-axis is called the **reference angle**.

steps.title: Watch the mirror work
steps.teach: Step from 30° up through 150°, 210°, 330°. The triangle under the point is IDENTICAL each time — only its quadrant changes. Watch the coordinates: same two numbers all four visits, wearing the quadrant's signs.

steps.title: Finding the reference angle
steps.body: Measure the acute gap between the terminal ray and the x-axis (NEVER the y-axis). Quadrant II: $180° - \theta$ (or $\pi - \theta$). Quadrant III: $\theta - 180°$. Quadrant IV: $360° - \theta$. Sanity: a reference angle is always between 0° and 90° — anything else means the wrong axis or the wrong quadrant.

steps.title: Three quick references
steps.prompt: The reference angles of $135°$, $\tfrac{4\pi}{3}$, and $300°$ are:

steps.title: Quadrants sign the values
steps.body: Second step, independent of the first: the quadrant hands out signs, straight from coordinates. QI: both positive. QII: x negative — cosine negative, sine positive. QIII: both negative. QIV: sine negative. That's it — you're just remembering where points are up/down and left/right. Size from the mirror, sign from the address.

steps.title: The 30° family, all four quadrants
steps.teach: The four cousins of 30° — at 30°, 150°, 210°, 330° — plotted on the unit circle. Same two coordinates $(0.87, 0.5)$ in size everywhere; each quadrant stamps its own signs. Identify each point's quadrant and the signs follow.

steps.title: Come home first
steps.body: Angles beyond a lap — $495°$, $-\frac{\pi}{6}$, $780°$ — describe the same points as some angle in $[0°, 360°)$: strip whole laps ($\pm 360°$, $\pm 2\pi$) until home. These **coterminal** angles share everything: $\sin 420° = \sin 60°$. Protocol: come home, find the quadrant, mirror, sign.

steps.title: Full protocol
steps.explanation: $\frac{11\pi}{6} = 330°$: QIV, reference $\frac{\pi}{6} = 30°$. Cosine in QIV is POSITIVE: $+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. Size from 30°, sign from the fourth quadrant.

steps.title: Say the two steps aloud
steps.body: Every evaluation, narrate: 'reference angle ___ , quadrant ___ , so sign ___.' For $\sin\frac{7\pi}{6}$: 'reference $\frac{\pi}{6}$, quadrant III, sine negative: $-\frac{1}{2}$.' Splitting size from sign out loud is what stops the classic merged-step errors — and it makes your work gradeable even when the final value slips.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Reference angle: acute gap to the x-axis — II: π−θ, III: θ−π, IV: 2π−θ.
Size from the reference, sign from the quadrant — two steps, never merged.
Oversized or negative? Strip full laps first (coterminal angles share the point).

practice.statement: Convert to radians: $60°$, $270°$, $-30°$; and to degrees: $\tfrac{3\pi}{2}$, $\tfrac{5\pi}{4}$.
A wiper blade of radius 40 cm sweeps $\tfrac{2\pi}{3}$ radians. What arc does its tip trace?

practice.solution: $(0,1)$: cos $= 0$. Full lap: sin $= 0$. Half lap either way lands at $(-1, 0)$: cos $= -1$. $(0,1)$: sin $= 1$.

practice.statement: $\sin\theta = \tfrac{8}{17}$ with $\theta$ in the second quadrant. Find $\cos\theta$.

practice.solution: QII, ref 30°: $-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. QIII, ref 45°: $-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$. QIV, ref $\frac{\pi}{3}$: $+\frac{1}{2}$.
Size: ref $= \frac{\pi}{3}$. Negative sine: QIII and QIV: $\theta = \pi + \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{4\pi}{3}$ and $\theta = 2\pi - \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{5\pi}{3}$.

testYourself.statement: Convert $\tfrac{11\pi}{6}$ to degrees and $315°$ to radians, then find both angles' reference angles.
A gear of radius 6 cm rotates $150°$. Find the arc traveled by a tooth, exactly.
$\cos\theta = \tfrac{7}{25}$ with $\theta$ in the fourth quadrant. Find $\sin\theta$, then verify the identity.
Order from smallest to largest without a calculator: $\sin 100°$, $\sin 150°$, $\sin 200°$.
testYourself.solution: $\sin 200°$ is NEGATIVE (QIII) — smallest. $\sin 150° = \frac{1}{2}$; $\sin 100°$ is just past the top, close to 1 — largest. Order: $\sin 200° < \sin 150° < \sin 100°$.
QII ref $\frac{\pi}{4}$: $+\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$. QII ref $\frac{\pi}{3}$: $-\frac{1}{2}$. QIV ref $\frac{\pi}{6}$: $-\frac{1}{2}$. Sum: $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} - 1$.

testYourself.statement: Find all $\theta$ in $[0°, 360°)$ with $\cos\theta = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, and all with $\sin\theta = -1$.
testYourself.solution: Positive cosine: QI and QIV with ref $45°$: $\theta = 45°, 315°$. $\sin\theta = -1$: only the bottom compass point: $\theta = 270°$.
